content.assessment: By leaving the selection age and track structure untouched, this proposal avoids the constitutional and primary-legislation hurdles that burden structural alternatives, operating instead through funding and administrative channels already within executive/ministerial competence. This makes it the lowest-legal-risk option, though it correspondingly leaves the rights and structural governance tensions entirely unaddressed.
opportunities: Existing non-structural legal channels, such as the 2023 teacher-career reform, already provide an operative statutory basis to expand equity-oriented funding without new legislation
strengths: Avoids the need for parliamentary amendment since it does not alter the codified 6/8-year track structure (F-live-legal_and_governance-01)
Sidesteps the constitutional free-choice conflict entirely since no admission or catchment restriction is proposed (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
threats: Because centralized operational control (curriculum, staffing, funding) already sits with the state, compensatory measures depend heavily on continued executive commitment and could be reversed or under-resourced without any legal guarantee of permanence, unlike a statutory structural change
weaknesses: Fiscal and quality levers alone cannot address the governance-level driver of outcomes: the highest EU socioeconomic segregation level and 53-point PISA gap are structural features tied to selection design, not solely funding gaps, so this lens finds the proposal's legal ease paired with limited leverage over the actual governance drivers (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
